coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on pepper and lettuce growth via irrigation enhancement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Salha, Belal Abu
Maruthapandi, Moorthy
Perelshtein, Ilana
Luong, John H.T.
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with an average diameter of 3.25 nm were prepared by a hydrothermal process from betel leaves as a single source of carbon and nitrogen. FTIR analysis attested to the presence of amine, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups in the NCD surface. The obtained aqueous solutions were applied to enhance the growth of pepper and lettuce plants in a greenhouse and a growing room, respectively. The growth of these two plants was observed and analyzed at different stages, indicating significantly higher fresh and dry weights of roots and peppers, compared to the control. The healthy growth results from hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs, and the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups provide abundant binding sites for water molecules, which penetrate the plant along with NCDs. It promotes the absorption and utilization of water and nutrients via ROS, which leads to improved nitrogen fixation. The supply of carbon dots stimulates and enhances the growth of both lettuce and pepper, illustrating the benefit of NCDs with minimal cytotoxicity in the enhanced production of other plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs), nano-fertilizer
Growth rate
Productivity
Agriculture

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and optical properties of NCDs
Effects of NCDs on lettuce and pepper growth
Mechanism of NCDs on plant growth and photosynthetic activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Prof. Aharon Gedanken's research laboratory at Bar Ilan University, for their valuable support and constant encouragement. We also thank Prof. Rafael Perl-Treves from the faculty of life science at Bar Ilan University for his plant growth support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of lettuce and pepper plants during growth in a greenhouse irrigated only with nutrients (W/N) and with CDs.
Photographs of lettuce and pepper plants in a growing room irrigated only with nutrients (W/N) and with NCDs.
(a) TEM (b) HR-TEM (c) cumulative particle distribution (d) photoluminescence spectra of NCDs.
(a) XPS full survey, (b–d) high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of NCDs.
Effect of NCDs (1.9 mg/mL) on the growth of lettuce plants: fresh and dried weights of (a, b) plants and (c, d) roots. All experiments were repeated at least three times. Asterisks denote p < 0.05.

Effect of CDs (1.9 mg/mL) on the growth of pepper plants: (a) number of fruit, (b) number of leaves, (c, d) fresh and dry weight, (e, f) fresh and dry leaf weight, and (g, h) fresh and dry root weight. All measurements were repeated at least three times. Asterisks denote p < 0.05.
Effect of NCDs (1.9 mg/mL) on the leaf area of pepper plants: (a) greenhouse, (b) growing room. All measurements were repeated at least three times. Asterisks denote p < 0.05.
A hydrothermal process for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from green betel leaves as a single source of carbon and nitrogen.
Effect of irrigation of lettuce & pepper plants with NCDs.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on pepper and lettuce growth via irrigation enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 5290002, Israel

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, University College Cork, Cork T12 YN60, Ireland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with an average diameter of 3.25 nm were prepared by a hydrothermal process from betel leaves as a single source of carbon and nitrogen. FTIR analysis attested to the presence of amine, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups in the NCD surface. The obtained aqueous solutions were applied to enhance the growth of pepper and lettuce plants in a greenhouse and a growing room, respectively. The growth of these two plants was observed and analyzed at different stages, indicating significantly higher fresh and dry weights of roots and peppers, compared to the control. The healthy growth results from hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs, and the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups provide abundant binding sites for water molecules, which penetrate the plant along with NCDs. It promotes the absorption and utilization of water and nutrients via ROS, which leads to improved nitrogen fixation. The supply of carbon dots stimulates and enhances the growth of both lettuce and pepper, illustrating the benefit of NCDs with minimal cytotoxicity in the enhanced production of other plants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data is available under request.

ce:section.ce:para: The world's population is expected to increase to around 10 billion people by 2050, and life expectancy along with food production and nutrient sufficiency are expected to gain increasing focus [1,2]. The cultivation of plant species (agriculture) constitutes one of the most important human endeavors in supplying food to the ecosystem, in particular to humankind [3]. Plants utilize light energy and nutrients absorbed from the environment for development and survival [4]. Increasing industrialization and infrastructure development are accelerating ecosystem destruction and contributing to global warming. Due to the destruction of agricultural land and plant species, the number of undernourished people who lack nutritional benefits from plants is growing [5]. Various technologies were developed to resolve this issue by enhancing agricultural production. Nonetheless, hunger levels remain alarmingly high worldwide [6]. Thus, the development of novel and sustainable strategies is gaining great importance, as they can enhance crop yield and increase nutrient efficiency [7]. However, conventional/synthesized fertilizers, lack of water availability, and limitations of crop resources restrict the cultivation of plants under marginal conditions [8]. The synthesized fertilizers (N, P, and K) help the plants to grow and adapt to the drought, however only a small amount of the nutrients can be taken, and the remaining inorganic minerals are unutilized [9]. The low nutrient conversion efficiency results in climate change, soil pollution, and groundwater pollution and affects plant growth [10,11]. To resolve these issues, the agriculture industry urgently needs to develop an environmentally friendly technology to face and encounter existing issues and increase food production, which can actively battle global hunger. Carbon-based nanomaterials have gained great attention due to addressing critical issues faced by agriculture [12]. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials with diverse applications ranging from optoelectronics to bioimaging and beyond [13]. CDs are the most promising carbon-based material possessing attractive properties such as low toxicity, small size, great biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, photostability, water solubility, low cost, eco-friendliness, simple preparation, and chemical inertness [14–16]. CDs are promising candidates in various fields including bio-sensing, bio-imaging, photocatalysis, and antibacterial and optoelectronic devices [17–19]. Using the natural abundance of biomass waste to convert novel carbon dots in an agriculture field has provided enormous environmental benefits and is cost-effective [20,21].

CDs with heteroatoms such as N, P, and K have been attempted as nano-fertilizers due to their good biocompatibility and reduced environmental toxicity [22]. Over the past few decades, CDs were developed for agriculture planting and nutrient generation [23]. Of note is the use of graphene quantum dots for culturing coriander and garlic plants. Such quantum dots helped improve the growth rate of these plants. Their concentration promotes photosynthesis and enhances the carbohydrate content in the plants [24]. Lei et al. developed N-doped and undoped CDs for phototoxicity, nutrient uptake, and translocation in mung beans. The N-doped dots exhibit excellent plant growth compared with undoped CDs, serving as a nano-fertilizer and generating nitrogen nutrients, which helps to promote plant growth [25]. However, the fabrication of heteroatom-doped CDs on a large scale is not yet utilized for the tremendous potential advantages in agriculture [26]. CDs support the growth of strawberries, resulting in high fruit yield and weight [21]. Moreover, the carbon dot-based nano fertilizer enhances plant yield without affecting soil fertility.

This study reports the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs), which are prepared from betel leaves, a single source of both carbon and nitrogen. The aqueous NCD solutions are used to support the growth of lettuce and pepper plants. The NCDs were irrigated for a month, then a series of experiments was conducted to follow the growth rate, yield, fresh and dried weight of the roots and leaves, and mortality rate. As the irrigation of the NCDs significantly improved plant growth as well as its products, they can be used as an excellent natural source of fertilizer to promote plant growth and fruit yield (Scheme 1).

Green leaves of Betel (Piper betel) were obtained from a local area in India [28]. Deionized (distilled) water was used to prepare carbon dots (CDs). All solvents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

Fresh betel leaves were washed with water and cut into small pieces, 5 g were transferred into a 150 mL Teflon tube, followed by the addition of 60 mL of water. After heating at 180 °C for 10 h, the resulting solution was filtered using a Whatman filter paper, centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min, and filtered (0.22 μm) with a syringe to remove larger particles [21].

CD morphology was observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM 2100, JEOL, Peabody, MA, USA). The sample preparation for TEM was prepared by dropping the aqueous solution of CDs onto a copper-coated TEM grid and drying at 50 °C for 2 h. Fluorescence spectrophotometers (Varian Cary Eclipse) were utilized to measure the optical characteristics. Elemental composition and functional groups of the N-CDs were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Nexsa spectrometer, England), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using an ALPHA-FTIR Quick Snap (Bruker, Germany) spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (32 scans, ATR, diamond at 45°).

The data presented for plant weight, root weight, fruit weight, leaf number, and leaf weight were analyzed using a one-way analysis of variance and compared with Duncan's test at a significant level of 0.05 in IBM-SPSS software (Version 21, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of freshly synthesized betel leaves NCDs were examined using HR-TEM measurements. The TEM images (Fig. 3a, b) revealed that the NCDs exhibited a consistent distribution of nearly spherical shapes highlighting the homogeneous nature of the NCDs. The particle diameters exhibited a relatively narrow range; with an average diameter of ∼3.25 nm, the pronounced peak around this average value indicates the prevalence of particles within this size range, underscoring the consistent nature of the synthesis process. Notably, these NCDs display a size distribution spanning from 1.5 to 5 nm (Fig. 3c) as confirmed using the particle analysis by Image J. The obtained photoluminescence spectra demonstrated the emitted light's characteristics depending on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3d). The spectra cover from 300 to 480 nm for the excitation wavelength (λex). As the excitation wavelength increases, there is a noticeable redshift in the emission wavelength, accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. In previous literature [9], the optimal emission peak occurs at 433 nm (λem) at the corresponding excitation wavelength of 360 nm (λex). This characteristic of excitation-based emission could potentially be attributed to variations in the sizes of the CDs during their preparation and the presence of different functional groups on their surfaces. In general, carbon dots exhibit diverse morphologies and particle sizes depending on the synthesis method and precursor material used. However, the average size range falls within the typical size range reported for carbon dots in many studies [31]. The spherical shape observed in the betel leaves NCDs is also a common morphology reported for carbon dots synthesized from different sources. Spherical or quasi-spherical shapes are often observed due to the quantum confinement effect in small carbon nanoparticles [32].

Fig. 4a represents the results of X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, which was conducted on betel leaves derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). In Fig. 4a, the full survey peaks at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV are attributed to the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s electronic states, respectively. The chemical bonds present within the NCDs were further investigated by analyzing the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra[33,34]. Fig. 4b provides an in-depth view of the deconvoluted C1s high-resolution spectra, featuring four distinct peaks at 284.1, 286.4, and 288.1 eV represent C–C/CC, C–N–C, C–O–C/C–OH, and OC–OH/OC–NH respectively [35]. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. 4c, the binding energy peaks at 399.6, 400.2, and 401.4 eV, indicative of the presence of CN, N–(C)3, and NH functional groups [36]. The deconvoluted peak (O1s) exhibits two predominant peaks at 531.6, and 533.1 eV, signifying the presence of OH, C–O–C, and CO groups (Fig. 4d). The occurrence of nitrogen and oxygen heteroatoms indicates the existence of large π-conjugated domains in the NCDs moiety.

FTIR spectroscopy was measured to validate the presence of functional groups and structural features in the NCDs, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . A significant and broad peak at 3210 cm−1 could be ascribed to the OH/NH stretching band [37]. The absorption band at 1568 cm−1 is assigned to the typical stretching peaks of the NH band. The peaks at ∼1375 cm−1 and 1296 cm−1 are related to the occurrence of CC stretching in phenolic/aromatic components and C–OH stretching within the carboxyl group [38,39]. Further FTIR analysis indicates the presence of amine, carbonyl, and hydroxyl functional groups in the NCD surface [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of NCDs on lettuce and pepper growth
ce:section.ce:para: Several studies have shown that Carbon dots (CDs) from various precursors such as chemicals and biomass for strawberries, mung bean sprouts, rice, grasses, peanut plants, and others, are without any threat to food security [27,41]. Compared with chemical-derived carbon dots, the naturally derived carbon dots have a good carbon content and various heteroatoms. In addition, the biomass-derived carbon dots showed very less cytotoxicity than chemically derived carbon dots. Importantly, the biomass-derived carbon dots are making soil balance in agriculture by adjusting the soil pH and introducing inorganic components (heteroatoms). To investigate the NCD nano-fertilizer ability in plant growth, the lettuce and pepper plants were chosen to be treated with betel-derived carbon dots. During one month of caring for the plants, the effect of the NCDs on the growth of lettuce plants in a greenhouse and a growing room was evaluated. The fresh and dry weights of lettuce plants irrigated with NCDs were higher than those exposed only to water and fertilizer as a control (Fig. 6a, b). The weights of fresh and dry lettuce roots follow a similar trend (Fig. 6c, d).

After two months of growing the pepper plants, we investigated the effect of NCDs as an effective fertilizer in the greenhouse and the growing room. The number of peppers in plants exposed to NCDs was higher compared to plants irrigated without dots (Fig. 7a). However, the number of leaves exhibited a noticeable difference between the greenhouse and the growing room. The number of leaves in the greenhouse was higher without NCDs, while the opposite trend was seen in the growing room (Fig. 7b). To evaluate the yield of pepper plants, fruit weights were measured. The fresh and dry pepper weights of plants irrigated with NCDs were higher compared with plants exposed to water and fertilizer as a control. This trend is much more pronounced in the greenhouse, as shown in Fig. 7c, d. In the greenhouse, the weight of the leaves of plants exposed to CDs and control fertilizer was similar even after drying, while in the growing room, the leaves of plants irrigated with CDs were significantly heavier (Fig. 7e, f). A similar trend was observed for the fresh and dry weight of the plant roots (Fig. 7g, h).

The leaf area of pepper plants irrigated with NCDs shows a slight difference compared to plants irrigated with nutrients and water in the greenhouse. However, the leaf area of pepper plants irrigated with NCDs in the growing room shows a significant difference compared to plants irrigated with nutrients. The leaf area of plants exposed to NCDs was 62.58 and 63.17 cm2, and the leaf area of plants exposed to nutrients and water was 59.65 and 54.56 cm2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8 . In the greenhouse, where all the plants receive water and nutrients, the leaf area of plants irrigated with NCDs shows a minor difference compared to those irrigated with nutrients and water. Nonetheless, the difference observed in the greenhouse might suggest that NCDs subtly influence pepper plants' growth and development under controlled conditions. In contrast, a significant difference in leaf area is observed when comparing plants irrigated with NCDs to those irrigated with only nutrients in the growing room. These results suggest that the application of NCDs in the growing room has a more pronounced impact on the leaf area of pepper plants compared to the use of nutrients alone.

The toxicity of the fertilizer is essential in the agriculture field. In a recent study, the concentration-dependent toxicity of carbon dots exceeded 125 mg mL−1 reducing the plant growth of Arabidopsis [42]. In our previous study, the betel-derived carbon dots enhanced the strawberries' growth and did not produce any toxicity on the strawberry plants. Furthermore, the effects of NCDs on plants have been reported including the chlorophyll content, biocompatibility, phenolic and flavonoid contents [21]. Recently, the plant growth enhancement effect of CDs has been studied for various plants such as mung bean, wheat, bean sprouts, lettuce, lentil seeds, rice, etc. is summarized in detail in Table S1. Most of the CDs have been utilized to grow the single-plant family. The biomass-derived NCDs have been successfully utilized as a nano fertilizer for multiple plant systems such as strawberries, lettuce, and pepper.

The toxicity of the fertilizer is essential in the agriculture field. In a recent study, the concentration-dependent toxicity of carbon dots exceeded 125 mg mL−1 reducing the plant growth of Arabidopsis [42]. In our previous study, the betel-derived carbon dots enhanced the strawberries' growth and did not produce any toxicity on the strawberry plants. Furthermore, the effects of NCDs on plants have been reported including the chlorophyll content, biocompatibility, phenolic and flavonoid contents [21]. Recently, the plant growth enhancement effect of CDs has been studied for various plants such as mung bean, wheat, bean sprouts, lettuce, lentil seeds, rice, etc. is summarized in detail in Table S1. Most of the CDs have been utilized to grow the single-plant family. The biomass-derived NCDs have been successfully utilized as a nano fertilizer for multiple plant systems such as strawberries, lettuce, and pepper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of NCDs on plant growth and photosynthetic activity
ce:section.ce:para: CDs known as an excellent light-harvesting capability can be utilized as artificial photo probes due to their photo-induced electron-donating and accepting ability. The plants can be used to increase the photosynthetic rate of leaves, thus stimulating plant growth. A light-conversion effect of amine-functionalized CDs prepared with betel leaves as precursors used as UV-blue conversion materials, increasing the photosynthetic rate of lettuce and pepper plants. Subsequently, our study prepared the NCDs from natural resources that absorbed UV radiation and emitted blue fluorescence. Growing lettuce and pepper with the CDs improved the electron transfer rate, photosynthetic efficacy, and photosynthetic activity. Previous studies have revealed that NCDs can increase the electron density in comparison with undoped CDs and decrease the recombination efficacy of electron-hole pairs, later providing electrons to the photosystem as electron donors. [43] The net photosynthetic rate was reduced by accelerating electron mobility. Nitrogen-doped CDs have broad luminescence because of the defect sites on the surface, which can stimulate plant growth [30]. CDs can improve the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and hole pairs in photosynthesis, increase the number of excitons, and expand the light harvest. CDs display great potential in improving the light harvest efficacy and light absorption range of plants and consequently, improving photosynthesis, but light intensity is also an important factor affecting crop photosynthesis. The plant photosynthetic rate differs with light intensity. Chloroplasts reabsorb light emitted by CDs with different quantum yields and concentrations for photosynthesis, which can increase the electron transfer rate in light reactions and generate more energy materials of ATP and NADPH for CO2 adjustments. [44] However, once the light intensity exceeds the light capacity point of photosynthesis in the leaves, the limited CO2 assimilation capacity limits the further improvement of photosynthetic efficiency, resulting in the transfer of excess photosynthetic electrons to oxygen and the generation of ROS, which causes damage to the photosynthetic system. Our NCDs contain various functional groups as well as different radicals on the surface, which can increase the photosynthesis properties of the material (Scheme 2 ). The functional groups of -COOH, -OH, -NH2, -CH2-NH, and -CO contains lone pair of electrons, this excess of electron involves the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and hole pairs during photosynthesis and increase the number of excitons and expand the light harvest. A more detailed mechanism of NCD irrigation was provided in our previous work [21].

The characterization of carbon dots' morphology, particle size, and photoluminescence properties provides valuable information for understanding their behavior and exploring their potential applications in photosynthetic fields. Our results obtained from the HR-TEM measurements of the freshly synthesized betel leaves NCDs provide valuable insights into their morphology and particle size. The consistent distribution of nearly spherical shapes indicates a uniform synthesis process, which is desirable for achieving consistent properties in the NCDs. The photoluminescence spectra reveal interesting characteristics of the emitted light depending on the excitation wavelength. The observed red shift in the emission wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength suggests that the NCDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior. Moreover, The presence of different functional groups on the NCD surfaces might contribute to the observed excitation-based emission characteristics. These functional groups could introduce energy levels or electronic states that affect the emission process, resulting in variations in the emitted light's characteristics.

The results presented indicate the effects of NCDs on the growth rate and productivity of lettuce and pepper plants. For lettuce plants, it was observed that irrigating them with NCDs led to higher fresh and dry weights compared to plants exposed only to water and fertilizer. This effect was also reflected in the weights of the lettuce roots. These findings suggest that the presence of NCDs positively influenced the growth and enhancement of lettuce plants. Regarding the observation of pepper plants, it was found that the number of peppers was higher in plants exposed to NCDs compared to those irrigated without NCDs. However, the number of leaves showed a noticeable difference between the greenhouse and the growing room. In the greenhouse, the number of leaves was higher without NCDs, whereas in the growing room, the opposite trend was observed. To evaluate the yield of pepper plants, the fresh and dry weights of the fruits were measured. The results exhibited that the plants irrigated with NCDs had higher pepper weights compared to the control group receiving only water and fertilizer. This effect was particularly pronounced in the greenhouse. As well, the weight of leaves and roots was examined. In the greenhouse, there was no pointed difference in the weight of leaves between plants exposed to NCDs and those receiving control fertilizer, even after drying. However, in the growing room, the leaves of plants irrigated with NCDs were significantly heavier. A similar development was observed for the fresh and dry weight of plant roots. These findings suggest that the application of NCDs as a fertilizer has a positive impact on the growth, yield, and weight of lettuce and pepper plants. The specific effects observed may vary depending on the environmental conditions, such as the greenhouse versus the growing room.

The research highlights the potential benefits of using NCDs to enhance plant growth and photosynthetic efficiency. By converting UV light into blue-violet or red light, NCDs can increase the photosynthetic rate of leaves, thereby stimulating plant growth. When lettuce and pepper plants were grown with these NCDs, the electron transfer rate, photosynthetic efficacy, and photosynthetic activity improved. The functional groups present on the surface of the NCDs, such as -COOH, -OH, -NH2, -CH2-NH, and -CO, contain lone pairs of electrons. These functional groups are believed to contribute to the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and hole pairs during photosynthesis, increasing the number of excitons and expanding the light harvest. Overall, the results suggest that NCDs hold promise for improving plant photosynthesis by enhancing light absorption and electron transfer processes. However, it is important to consider factors such as optimal light intensity to ensure the proper functioning of the photosynthetic system and prevent potential damage. When we compare CDs to other nanoparticles employed in agriculture, NCDs stand out due to their exceptional water solubility, uniform particle size, and stable fluorescence emission. These qualities not only facilitate their application but also ensure their efficient uptake by plants, promoting enhanced nutrient absorption and growth. Unlike some other nanoparticles that might exhibit aggregation or instability over time, the stable fluorescence emission and active functional groups of NCDs underscore their reliability and sustained effectiveness in improving plant vitality.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) prepared on a large scale by hydrothermal process enabled superior growth of pepper and lettuce conducted in parallel in a greenhouse and in a growing room. The obtained NCDs exhibit good water solubility, mono-dispersity, strong and stable fluorescence emission, and active functional groups in agreement with our previous research on strawberry growth. The study indicates that the application of NCDs as a fertilizer positively influences the growth, yield, and weight of lettuce and pepper plants. When irrigated with NCDs, lettuce plants exhibited higher fresh and dry weights, as well as enhanced root development. Pepper plants exposed to NCDs had a higher number of peppers and increased fruit weights compared to the control group. The research also highlights the potential benefits of NCDs in improving plant photosynthesis by increasing the photosynthetic rate, electron transfer rate, and photosynthetic efficacy. Overall, these findings suggest that NCDs have the potential to be a valuable tool for enhancing plant growth and productivity.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Table S1
Overview of the growth enhancement effect of CDs in plants as a nano-fertilizer.
Table S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.111998.

Belal Abu Salha: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Moorthy Maruthapandi: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Ilana Perelshtein: Project administration, Formal analysis. John H.T. Luong: Visualization, Software. Aharon Gedanken: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation.
There is no funding for this research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Prof. Aharon Gedanken's research laboratory at Bar Ilan University, for their valuable support and constant encouragement. We also thank Prof. Rafael Perl-Treves from the faculty of life science at Bar Ilan University for his plant growth support.

Lancet

Journal of Future Foods

Front. Plant Sci.

Biol. Res.

Li, Fate of nitrogen in agriculture and environment: agronomic, eco-physiological and molecular approaches to improve nitrogen use efficiency, Biol Res 53 (2020). doi:10.1186/s40659-020-00312-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the applications of nano-agrochemicals for sustainable agricultural development
